nativeRange.habitat: Found in sluggish tributaries, small rivers, swampy areas, and flooded forest lakes with dense aquatic vegetation, submerged roots, leaf litter, and woody debris. These habitats are typically heavily shaded, with blackwater or clearwater conditions produced by decomposing organic matter.

decorPreferences: dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, floating plants, water sprite)
dim lighting or surface cover to reduce light intensity

diet.notes: A micropredator in the wild, the Three-Lined Pencilfish feeds on tiny invertebrates, worms, crustaceans, and insects. In the aquarium it generally accepts high-quality micro-pellets and finely crushed flake as a staple, supplemented regularly with small live and frozen foods such as baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Moina, Daphnia, cyclops, and grindal worm. Because the mouth is very small, standard adult-sized flake should always be crushed to a fine powder before offering.

goodWith: small peaceful characins (ember tetras, neon tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) as dither fish
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish (outcompete at feeding)
shrimp (small shrimp may be picked at)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Nannostomus trifasciatus; verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: A slender, jewel-toned blackwater pencilfish from the Amazon basin distinguished by three bold horizontal stripes and a dramatic nocturnal colour switch to cryptic vertical barring. Best kept in soft, acidic water in groups of 8 or more; an intermediate-level species ideal for blackwater biotope setups.
careNotes: The Three-Lined Pencilfish is a delicate, small-mouthed characin demanding soft, acidic water — ideally pH 5.5–7.0 with very low hardness (gH 0–7). In harder or alkaline tap water the species becomes stressed, colour fades, and disease susceptibility rises; RO water blended with blackwater extract (Indian almond leaf, peat filtration, or commercial tannin products) is strongly recommended in hard-water regions. Keep in groups of at least 8–10: males display and chase conspecific rivals, and larger shoals distribute aggression while promoting natural behaviour. Males are more intensely coloured with a reddish flush on the lower body; females are plumper when gravid. Sponge or air-driven filtration is preferable — the species dislikes strong current. Tank must be covered tightly as this species is an escape risk. Dim lighting replicates the shaded forest-stream habitat and draws out the richest colouration. Leaf litter, driftwood, and fine-leaved plants recreate the biotope and provide resting sites and spawning surfaces. Minimum footprint of 75 × 30 cm (≈60 L) is adequate for a species-only group; 80 × 40 cm or larger is preferred for mixed community setups. Three-Lined Pencilfish are often compared to Beckford's Pencilfish (N. beckfordi): both share the three-stripe pattern, but trifasciatus is more demanding of soft-acidic water and is notably less forgiving of hardness or pH drift. See also: [beckfords-pencilfish].
breedingNotes: Breeding is possible in a dedicated 40–60 L tank but is considered moderately difficult and is seldom achieved commercially. Condition pairs or a small group on live foods (Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, Moina) for 1–2 weeks before a breeding attempt. Use very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, near-zero hardness) at a slightly elevated temperature (26–28°C). Dense mops of Java moss or fine-leaved plants serve as spawning medium; eggs are adhesive and scattered on plant surfaces. A typical spawning produces 30–100 eggs. Eggs hatch in 24–72 hours depending on temperature; fry become free-swimming 3–4 days later. Fry are extremely small and require infusoria or very fine commercial first foods such as rotifers for the first week, transitioning to baby brine shrimp nauplii thereafter. Parents do not display strong egg-eating behaviour if well-fed, but can be removed after spawning for certainty. Raise fry in very soft water and avoid disturbance.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus trifasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes), native range (Brazil, Guyana, Peru, Colombia, Bolivia), habitat (sluggish tributaries, blackwater, flooded forest lakes), water parameters (22–28°C, pH 4.0–7.5, hardness 18–215 ppm), adult size (30–33 mm SL), minimum tank 75×30 cm / 68 L, diet (micropredator; Artemia nauplii, Moina, grindal worm), grouping (10+), day/night colour change to vertical cryptic barring, breeding difficulty (moderate, egg scatterer, 30–100 eggs, 24–72 h hatch). Characterised as beginner-friendly among pencilfishes, though soft-water requirement implies intermediate for general aquarists.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus trifasciatus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes, subfamily Pyrrhulininae), native range (Peruvian/Bolivian/Brazilian Amazon, Negro River basin, Guyana), adult size 3.6 cm SL, temperature 24–28°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness up to 4 dH. Size spread: SeriouslyFish 30–33 mm SL vs FishBase 3.6 cm max — consistent; used 3.0–3.6 cm range. pH spread: SeriouslyFish 4.0–7.5 (wild tolerance) vs FishBase 5.5–7.0 (captive optimum); used broader wild range per SeriouslyFish with note on captive preference.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General pencilfish care guide covering Nannostomus genus. Temperature 24–27°C, pH 6.5–7.5 (slightly more conservative than SeriouslyFish). Minimum 10 gal (38 L) for small species, 20–29 gal preferred for groups. Emphasised tiny mouth requiring crushed flake, live baby brine shrimp, cyclops. Notes shrimp predation risk. No trifasciatus-specific day/night colour change mentioned. Conservative pH of 6.5 noted vs SeriouslyFish 4.0 lower bound — likely reflecting broad genus guidance rather than species-specific minimum.

additional.name: aqua-fish.net — Threestripe Pencilfish
additional.notes: Provided tank size (57 L / 15 gal minimum), temperature 23–29°C, pH 5.9–7.0, gH 0–7°, lifespan ~5 years, adult size 4–5 cm (slightly larger than FishBase SL — likely TL with fin tips; used FishBase 3.6 cm SL as authoritative upper bound). Confirmed diet (crushed flake, Daphnia, brine shrimp), peaceful schooling temperament, group of 6+ advised.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Three-lined Pencilfish
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 24–28°C, pH 5.5–7.0, kH up to 8, adult size 4 cm, soft-acidic water requirement. Noted cryptic overnight colour change to pale/banded pattern. Breeding described as challenging, up to 100 eggs, 36–72 h hatch, fry free-swimming at 3–4 days. Diet: flake, micropellets, small frozen foods (bloodworm, white mosquito larvae, daphnia). Escape risk flagged (tight coverslides recommended).

fish.breedingNotes: The Three-Lined Pencilfish is an egg-scattering free spawner that deposits 30–100 adhesive eggs on fine-leaved plants per spawning. Eggs typically hatch within 24–72 hours, and fry become free-swimming around 3–4 days after hatching. Successful breeding requires very soft, acidic conditioning water (pH 5.5–6.5) and thorough preparation on live foods. The species is seldom bred commercially and is generally considered an intermediate breeding project.
